# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**Define a stimulation microelectrode array. List any two commonly used electrode materials for chronic neural stimulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stimulation microelectrode array** is an ordered assembly of micro-scale conductive electrodes fabricated on a rigid or flexible substrate, designed to deliver controlled electrical charge across the electrode–tissue interface so as to depolarize neuronal membranes and elicit action potentials.

Two commonly used electrode materials for chronic stimulation are:
1. **Platinum (Pt)** — used in cochlear implants due to its excellent biocompatibility and corrosion resistance.
2. **Platinum–Iridium (Pt-Ir 90:10)** — used in DBS leads; combines Pt's biocompatibility with Ir's mechanical strength.

*(Any other valid material: Ir/IrOx, TiN, PEDOT, Pt black. Award 1 mark for the definition and 1 mark each for the two materials.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**

**What is the "water window" of an electrode, and why is it important for neural stimulation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **water window** is the range of electrode potentials — typically from about **−0.6 V to +0.8 V (vs Ag/AgCl)** — within which water does **not** undergo electrolysis to produce hydrogen or oxygen gas.

It is important because:
1. A stimulation electrode must operate **entirely within this window** to avoid gas evolution, which mechanically damages tissue and shifts pH.
2. It sets the **maximum reversible charge** that can be safely injected; the cathodic charge storage capacity (CSCc) measured within this window defines the safe charge-injection limit.

*(Award 1 mark for the range/value, 2 marks for the two reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Classify stimulation microelectrode arrays based on their **structural geometry**, and describe the construction and features of the **Utah Array** and the **Michigan Probe** with suitable applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the electrode materials **Pt, Pt-Ir, IrOx, and PEDOT** in terms of **(i) charge injection capacity, (ii) impedance, (iii) biocompatibility, and (iv) clinical status**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Classification of Stimulation MEAs by Geometry (3 marks):**

| Geometry Type | Examples |
|---------------|----------|
| **3-D silicon needle array** | Utah Array |
| **2-D planar / shank probe** | Michigan Probe |
| **Microwire bundle** | Thornton Array |
| **Polymer-based flexible** | Parylene-C, Polyimide arrays |
| **Nerve cuff / sieve** | Cuff electrodes, regenerative sieve electrodes |

**Utah Array (2 marks):**

- Developed by **Richard Normann's group at the University of Utah** in the 1990s.
- Comprises a **10 × 10 (or up to 100 × 100) array** of tapered silicon needles, each approximately **1.0–1.5 mm long** with **tip diameters of 5–50 µm**.
- Each needle has a **single active electrode at its tip** (typically Pt or Pt-Ir sputtered); the rest of the needle is insulated (e.g., parylene-C or SiO₂).
- Fabrication uses **bulk micromachining** of a silicon wafer with dicing saws or chemical etching.
- **Application:** Cortical prosthesis — motor cortex recording/stimulation for **brain–computer interfaces (BCIs)** (e.g., the BrainGate trial), visual cortex stimulation.

**Michigan Probe (2 marks):**

- Developed at the **University of Michigan** (Wise, Najafi, Bhatt).
- A **planar / 2-D structure**: thin silicon shanks (15–50 µm wide × 15 µm thick × a few mm long) fabricated by **surface micromachining** using photolithography and reactive-ion etching (RIE).
- Each shank carries **multiple electrode sites along its length** (typically 4, 8, 16, or 32 sites), allowing recording/stimulation at different depths.
- Multi-shank variants exist with 2, 4, or more shanks per probe.
- **Application:** Cortical and deep-brain neural recording; hippocampal recording in animal studies; research tool for neuroscience.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Comparison Table (7 marks — award approx 1.5 marks per material, distributed across the four criteria):**

| Criterion | Pt | Pt-Ir (90:10) | IrOx (SIROF) | PEDOT:PSS |
|-----------|----|---------------|--------------|-----------|
| **(i) Charge injection capacity** | 100–300 µC/cm² | 100–350 µC/cm² | 1000–4000 µC/cm² | 1000–15000 µC/cm² (claimed) |
| **(ii) Impedance @ 1 kHz (for ~1000 µm² site)** | 50–200 kΩ | 50–180 kΩ | 5–30 kΩ | 1–10 kΩ |
| **(iii) Biocompatibility** | Excellent | Excellent | Good (long-term clinical use) | Good in short-term; chronic stability under study |
| **(iv) Clinical status (FDA-approved chronic use)** | Yes — Cochlear, DBS, SCS | Yes — DBS leads, cochlear | Yes — Argus II retinal implant | No (research only as of 2024) |

**Summary statement (1 bonus mark for clear conclusion):**
Pt and Pt-Ir remain the clinical gold standard for chronic stimulation due to their unmatched long-term stability. IrOx offers a 10× higher charge capacity and is used where high charge is required (retina). PEDOT offers the highest charge capacity but lacks long-term clinical validation.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern, Numerical Emphasis]**

**(a)** Define **geometric charge density** and the **Shannon safety limit** for neural stimulation. A Pt-Ir microelectrode with an active site of area $A_{\text{geo}} = 2000 \,\mu\text{m}^2$ delivers a rectangular, charge-balanced biphasic current pulse of amplitude $I = 150 \,\mu\text{A}$ and pulse width $t_p = 200 \,\mu\text{s}$. Calculate **(i)** the charge per phase, **(ii)** the geometric charge density in both $\text{C/m}^2$ and $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$, and **(iii)** verify whether the pulse satisfies Shannon's limit for $k = 1.7$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **water window**? Why are **biphasic charge-balanced pulses** mandatory for chronic neural stimulation? Explain with reference to **(i) electrochemical product accumulation, (ii) electrode corrosion, and (iii) electrolysis of tissue water**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Geometric charge density $D$** is the charge delivered per unit phase divided by the geometric (physical) surface area of the active electrode site: $D = Q / A_{\text{geo}}$. It is the primary safety metric in stimulation.
- **Shannon safety limit** is an empirical rule stating that for safe chronic stimulation, $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le k$, where $D$ is in $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ and $Q$ is in $\mu\text{C}$ per phase; typical $k = 1.5$ to $1.85$.

**Computation (5 marks):**

**(i) Charge per phase:**

$$
Q = I \cdot t_p = (150 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}) \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s})
$$

$$
Q = 150 \times 200 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C} = 30{,}000 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 3 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{C} = 30 \,\text{nC}}
$$

**(ii) Geometric charge density in SI units:**

$$
A_{\text{geo}} = 2000 \,\mu\text{m}^2 = 2000 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^2
$$

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{geo}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}\,\text{C/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 15 \,\text{C/m}^2}
$$

**Conversion to µC/cm² (1 C/m² = 100 µC/cm²):**

$$
D = 15 \times 100 = 1500 \,\mu\text{C/cm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 1500 \,\mu\text{C/cm}^2 = 1.5 \,\text{mC/cm}^2}
$$

**(iii) Shannon verification:**

Convert $Q$ to µC: $Q = 30 \,\text{nC} = 0.03 \,\mu\text{C}$.

$$
\log_{10}(D) = \log_{10}(1500) = \log_{10}(1.5 \times 10^3) = \log_{10}(1.5) + 3 \approx 0.176 + 3 = 3.176
$$

$$
\log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}(0.03) = \log_{10}(3 \times 10^{-2}) = \log_{10}(3) - 2 \approx 0.477 - 2 = -1.523
$$

$$
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) = 3.176 + (-1.523) = 1.653
$$

Since $1.653 \le 1.7$:

$$
\boxed{\text{The pulse SATISFIES Shannon's limit (just barely).}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Warning:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** in $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$: students commonly compute $D$ correctly in $\text{C/m}^2$ but forget to convert to $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ before plugging into Shannon's formula. **Always convert first.**
> 2. **Confusing pulse width with period:** $t_p$ is the **per-phase pulse width**, not the inter-pulse interval.
> 3. **Charge per phase vs. total charge:** for a biphasic pulse, $Q$ refers to **one phase only** (the cathodic phase).
> 4. **Geometric vs. electrochemical area:** Shannon uses $A_{\text{geo}}$, not $A_{\text{ESA}}$. Using ESA would give a falsely low $D$ and is incorrect.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Water Window (2 marks):**

The water window is the range of electrode potentials over which the aqueous electrolyte does **not** undergo hydrolysis. For physiological saline at body temperature, it spans approximately:

$$
E_{\text{window}} \approx -0.6 \,\text{V to} +0.8 \,\text{V vs Ag/AgCl}
$$

Beyond this range, water is reduced (cathodic limit, H₂ evolution) or oxidized (anodic limit, O₂ evolution, chloride oxidation to Cl₂, and dissolution of the metal itself).

**Why Biphasic Charge-Balanced Pulses Are Mandatory (5 marks, ≈ 1.5 marks each):**

**(i) Electrochemical Product Accumulation:**
A monophasic pulse leaves a net DC charge at the electrode–tissue interface, generating reactive oxidation or reduction products (e.g., H₂O₂, Cl₂, metal ions). These accumulate in tissue and trigger inflammation, gliosis, and neurotoxicity. A biphasic pulse reverses the half-reactions so that products of the first phase are consumed by the second.

**(ii) Electrode Corrosion:**
DC currents drive net oxidation of the metal at the anode phase, dissolving the electrode over time and changing its impedance and geometry. Charge-balanced biphasic stimulation prevents net metal dissolution, dramatically extending device lifetime (years to decades).

**(iii) Electrolysis of Tissue Water:**
Pure DC current splits water into H₂ and O₂ gas bubbles, which mechanically disrupt neural tissue, shift local pH (alkaline at cathode, acidic at anode), and produce toxic free radicals. Charge-balanced biphasic pulses minimize this — strictly zero DC current means no sustained electrolysis.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The clinical requirement is therefore **charge-balanced biphasic stimulation** — with **active DC-blocking circuitry** in addition, because real electrodes have asymmetric access resistance and a small residual DC offset can otherwise build up over millions of pulses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Stimulation Microelectrode Arrays**

**Conceptual anchors:**
- A stimulation MEA is a **"bed of nails"** of microelectrodes designed to inject charge across the electrode–tissue interface to depolarize neurons.
- Three classification axes: **(i) geometry**, **(ii) substrate**, **(iii) charge-transfer mechanism**.

**Geometry must-know list:**
- **Utah Array** = 3-D silicon needle bundle, used in cortical BCI.
- **Michigan Probe** = 2-D planar silicon shank, used for depth-resolved recording/stimulation.
- **Microwire (Thornton)** = bundled insulated metal wires for low-cost chronic recording.
- **Polymer flexible (Parylene-C)** = low Young's modulus for chronic cortical use.
- **Cuff / Sieve** = peripheral nerve interface.

**Materials must-know list:**
- **Pt, Pt-Ir** — clinical gold standard for chronic stimulation.
- **Ir/IrOx (SIROF)** — highest reversible charge capacity among FDA-approved; used in Argus II retinal prosthesis.
- **TiN** — high CSC, used as coating on cochlear electrodes.
- **Au** — recording only, not for high-density stimulation.
- **W (tungsten)** — acute microwire only; toxic corrosion products prevent chronic use.
- **PEDOT, PPy** — research-grade conductive polymers with very high CSC but unproven long-term stability.

**Equations to memorize verbatim:**
- $Q = I \cdot t_p$
- $D = Q / A_{\text{geo}}$
- $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le k$, with $D$ in $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$, $Q$ in $\mu\text{C}$, $k \approx 1.5$–$2.0$.
- $Z(\omega) = R_s + R_{ct} / (1 + j \omega R_{ct} C_{dl})$
- Charge balance: $I_c \cdot t_{p,c} = I_a \cdot t_{p,a}$.

**Water window:** approximately **−0.6 V to +0.8 V vs Ag/AgCl**.

**Pulse design rule:** Always **biphasic**, **charge-balanced**, with **inter-phase gap** and **DC-blocking capacitor** for chronic clinical use.

**Clinical-approved devices to mention in answers:**
- Cochlear implants (Cochlear, Med-El) — Pt/Pt-Ir ring electrodes.
- Argus II retinal prosthesis — SIROF-coated Pt array.
- DBS (Medtronic Activa) — Pt-Ir cylindrical contacts.
- BrainGate BCI — Utah Array.

> [!TIP]
> **Topper's Strategy:** In every MEA question, structure your answer as **(i) definition, (ii) classification, (iii) material choice with justification, (iv) safety limit (Shannon), (v) one numerical example or clinical device**. This 5-part structure covers any conceivable 7- or 14-mark question on Module 1.

topics.title: Optic Nerve Stimulation Using Penetrating Microelectrode Arrays
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Optic Nerve Stimulation Using Penetrating Microelectrode Arrays

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optic Nerve Stimulation Using Penetrating Microelectrode Arrays (OPN-PMA)** is a *visual neuroprosthetic strategy* in which arrays of fine, needle-like microelectrodes are surgically inserted transversely (perpendicular to the longitudinal axis) into the **optic nerve (CN II)** to deliver biphasic, charge-balanced electrical current pulses directly to the **retinal ganglion cell (RGC) axons**, thereby eliciting patterned *phosphenes* — the fundamental building blocks of artificial vision in blind patients suffering from outer retinal degenerative diseases (e.g., Retinitis Pigmentosa, Age-related Macular Degeneration) where the inner retinal circuitry and optic nerve remain functionally viable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic sits at the intersection of *neural interface engineering* and *sensory substitution*. The optic nerve is one of the **three extra-cortical visual interfaces** (retina, optic nerve, LGN) studied in Module 1, distinguished by its ability to elicit phosphenes even when the entire retina is degenerated — provided the RGC axons remain intact.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bundled Telephone Cable"

Imagine the **optic nerve as a massive underground telephone cable** containing roughly **1.2 million individual insulated wires (axons)**, each carrying a different pixel of visual information from the eye to the brain's visual processing centre.

- A **camera-based encoder** outside the cable is the *external image processor* (like a head-mounted camera in a visual prosthesis).
- A **penetrating microelectrode array** is like a *bed of surgical needles* that pierces the cable sheath and makes direct contact with small, specific bundles of these wires.
- When the system sends a tiny electrical pulse to one specific needle, it activates a *localized cluster of wires*, producing a single "ping" of perceived light — a **phosphene**.
- By carefully controlling **which needles fire, in what order, and with what intensity**, the device can "draw" simple shapes (letters, edges) inside the patient's conscious visual field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core takeaway:** Unlike epiretinal/subretinal implants that depend on a viable inner retina, optic nerve stimulation bypasses the retina entirely — it activates the axons *downstream* of the photoreceptors, making it a candidate for end-stage blindness.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & System Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|
| Number of axons in human optic nerve | **~1.2 × 10⁶** |
| Optic nerve diameter | **2 – 3 mm** at retrobulbar region |
| RGC axon diameter | **0.5 – 2.5 μm** |
| Penetrating electrode shaft length | **1.0 – 1.5 mm** |
| Typical electrode tip diameter | **5 – 15 μm** |
| Single-pulse charge per phase | **0.05 – 1.0 μC** |
| Pulse width | **50 – 500 μs** |
| Stimulation frequency | **50 – 250 Hz** |
| Reported phosphene threshold (current) | **10 – 200 μA** |
| Number of electrodes in clinical arrays | **10 – 100** (e.g., C-Sight, 20-ch) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical Foundation — Why the Optic Nerve?

The visual pathway is a four-tier serial relay:

$$\text{Photoreceptors (Rods/Cones)} \longrightarrow \text{Bipolar Cells} \longrightarrow \text{RGCs} \longrightarrow \text{Optic Nerve} \longrightarrow \text{LGN} \longrightarrow \text{V1 Cortex}$$

In **Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP)**, the outermost tier (rods/cones) degenerates, but RGC axons and their cell bodies in the **ganglion cell layer** survive for decades. This anatomical *rescue window* motivates direct electrical interfacing with the optic nerve.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Penetrating Stimulation

The system can be decomposed into seven functional stages, each governed by a specific engineering or biophysical principle.

1. **Image Acquisition** — A head-mounted or spectacle-mounted CMOS camera captures the external scene.
2. **Pre-processing** — The image is converted to a low-resolution grayscale (typically 10 × 10 to 32 × 32 pixels) suitable for the limited electrode count.
3. **Phosphene Mapping** — Each pixel is mapped to a single electrode through a *phosphene-position calibration matrix* established in early clinical sessions.
4. **Stimulus Synthesis** — A biphasic, charge-balanced, cathodic-first current pulse is generated by a *current-controlled stimulator* (voltage-controlled sources are unsafe due to electrochemical drift at the electrode-tissue interface).
5. **Microelectrode Delivery** — Penetrating microelectrodes (Utah or Michigan-style) deliver the charge directly to the **intraneural compartment** of the optic nerve.
6. **Axonal Recruitment** — Supra-threshold current depolarizes the axonal membrane, generating action potentials that propagate orthodromically toward the LGN and antidromically back toward the now-degenerated retina (irrelevant for perception).
7. **Cortical Readout** — The brain interprets the spatially-patterned action-potential volley as a "pixelated" visual percept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why penetrating (not cuff) electrodes?** A *cuff electrode* wraps around the epineurium and can only inject current that must diffuse through the perineurium and endoneurium, exciting only superficial axons. A **penetrating array** reaches the **central core fascicles**, allowing far lower thresholds (μA range vs. mA range for cuffs) and a more spatially-restricted activation volume.

### 2.3 Critical Biophysical Mechanisms

#### (a) Membrane Depolarization
When extracellular current density exceeds the **rheobase** of the local axonal membrane, voltage-gated sodium channels open, producing an action potential.

$$\frac{dV_m}{dt} = \frac{1}{C_m}\left(I_{stim} - I_{Na} - I_K - I_L\right)$$

where $C_m$ is the specific membrane capacitance (~1 μF/cm² for mammalian axon), and $I_{stim}$ is the externally injected current.

#### (b) Strength–Duration Relationship
The threshold charge is governed by the **Weiss–Lapicque equation**:

$$I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

where $I_{rh}$ is the *rheobase current*, $t$ is pulse width, and $\tau_m$ is the membrane time constant (~50–200 μs for RGC axons).

For very short pulses ($t \ll \tau_m$), the *charge threshold* becomes approximately constant:

$$Q_{th} \approx I_{rh} \cdot \tau_m$$

#### (c) Shannon Equation (Safety Limit)
To avoid electrochemical tissue damage, the **charge per phase (Q)** and **charge density (D)** must obey the empirical Shannon safety constraint:

$$\log_{10}(D) + k \cdot \log_{10}(Q) \leq M$$

Typical KTU-acceptable values: $k = 1.5$, $M = 1.85$, with $D$ in μC/cm²·phase and $Q$ in μC/phase.

#### (d) Activation Function
The likelihood of axon recruitment at any point in the tissue is described by the *activating function* derived from the second spatial derivative of the extracellular potential:

$$f(x) = \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_e(x)}{\partial x^2}$$

Maximum recruitment occurs where $\Phi_e$ has the steepest spatial gradient — i.e., **at the electrode tip**, which is precisely why tip geometry dominates device performance.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and magnitude operators are written as `\vert` or `\mid` so that the markdown table pipes are not corrupted.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weiss–Lapicque threshold | $I_{th} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$ | μA | Sets minimum current for given pulse width |
| 2 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \cdot t_{pw}$ | μC | Safety & perception parameter |
| 3 | Charge density | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}}$ | μC/cm² | Electrode-tissue damage limit |
| 4 | Shannon safety limit | $\log_{10}D + 1.5\,\log_{10}Q \le 1.85$ | dimensionless | Chronic safety compliance |
| 5 | Membrane time constant | $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ | ms | Defines chronaxie |
| 6 | Chronaxie time | $t_{chr} = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m$ | μs | Optimum stimulation pulse width |
| 7 | Activating function | $f = \partial^2 \Phi_e / \partial x^2$ | V/m² | Predicts recruitment site |
| 8 | Electrode impedance | $Z = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{dl}}$ at low ω | Ω (at 1 kHz) | Voltage compliance budget |
| 9 | Double-layer capacitance | $C_{dl} \approx 10 - 20 \,\mu F/cm^2$ | μF/cm² | Typical for Ir/IrOx, TiN |
| 10 | Tissue spread function | $V(r) = \dfrac{I}{4\pi \sigma r}$ | V | Approx. monopolar decay |
| 11 | Stimulation frequency | $f = 50 - 250$ | Hz | Phosphene refresh rate |
| 12 | Number of phosphenes per second | $N_{pps} = N_{el} \cdot f \cdot t_{frame}$ | — | Information bandwidth |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Strength–Duration Curve (Weiss–Lapicque Model)

The derivation is requested frequently in KTU Module 1 numerical sub-parts. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step transition.

**Starting Premise — Passive Membrane Equation:**
The axonal membrane, in the sub-threshold regime, behaves as a parallel RC circuit obeying:

$$C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \frac{V_m}{R_m} = I_{stim}(t)$$

where $C_m$ is the lumped membrane capacitance, $R_m$ is the membrane resistance, and $I_{stim}(t)$ is the applied intracellular-style stimulus (here, intracellularly induced by the extracellular field).

**Step 1 — Define Membrane Time Constant**
Divide both sides by $1/R_m$:

$$R_m C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + V_m = R_m I_{stim}(t)$$

Substitute the standard notation $\tau_m = R_m C_m$:

$$\tau_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + V_m = R_m I_{stim}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply a Rectangular Pulse of Amplitude I₀ and Duration t**
For $0 \le \tau \le t$, $I_{stim} = I_0$ (constant).

The first-order linear ODE admits the solution:

$$V_m(\tau) = I_0 R_m \left(1 - e^{-\tau/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Threshold Condition**
An action potential is fired when the membrane voltage reaches the threshold $V_{th}$ at the end of the pulse ($\tau = t$):

$$V_{th} = I_0 R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Threshold Current**
Rearranging:

$$I_{th} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce Rheobase**
Define the *rheobase* as the minimum current that can fire the axon for an infinitely long pulse:

$$I_{rh} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m}$$

Substituting yields the **Weiss–Lapicque equation**:

$$I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

**Step 6 — Short-Pulse Approximation (Charge Threshold)**
For pulses much shorter than the membrane time constant ($t \ll \tau_m$), the Taylor expansion $e^{-t/\tau_m} \approx 1 - t/\tau_m$ gives:

$$1 - e^{-t/\tau_m} \approx \frac{t}{\tau_m}$$

Therefore:

$$I_{th} \approx I_{rh} \cdot \frac{\tau_m}{t}$$

Multiplying both sides by $t$:

$$Q_{th} = I_{th} \cdot t \approx I_{rh} \cdot \tau_m = \text{constant}$$

This is the **charge-threshold regime**, foundational to all charge-balanced neural stimulation.

**Step 7 — Long-Pulse Approximation (Rheobase Limit)**
For $t \to \infty$, $e^{-t/\tau_m} \to 0$, so:

$$I_{th} \to I_{rh}$$

**Step 8 — Chronaxie Time**
The pulse width at which $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$ is defined as the **chronaxie time** $t_c$:

$$2 = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_c = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m}$$

**Final Consolidated Strength–Duration Expression:**

$$\boxed{\,I_{th}(t) = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - 2^{-t/t_c}}\,}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** An optic-nerve penetrating electrode has $I_{rh} = 30 \,\mu A$ and $\tau_m = 120 \,\mu s$. Find (a) the threshold current for a 200 μs pulse, and (b) the chronaxie time.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Use the Weiss–Lapicque equation:

$$I_{th} = \frac{30 \,\mu A}{1 - e^{-200/120}}$$

$$e^{-200/120} = e^{-1.6667} = 0.1889$$

$$I_{th} = \frac{30}{1 - 0.1889} = \frac{30}{0.8111} \approx 36.98 \,\mu A$$

**(b)** Chronaxie:

$$t_c = 0.693 \times 120 \,\mu s = 83.16 \,\mu s$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Charge-Density Safety (Shannon Form)

The Shannon equation is an empirical envelope fit to many in-vivo histopathology studies. Its standard derivation begins from the linear regression of safe operating points on a $\log D$ vs. $\log Q$ plane.

Assume an electrochemical damage boundary of the form:

$$D^a \cdot Q^b = K$$

where $a$ and $b$ are fitted exponents. Taking $\log_{10}$:

$$a \log_{10} D + b \log_{10} Q = \log_{10} K$$

With the convention $a = 1$, $b = k$, $M = \log_{10} K$:

$$\log_{10} D + k \log_{10} Q \le M$$

For Pt-Ir microelectrodes with IrOx coating: $k = 1.5$, $M = 1.85$ is the most clinically cited bound.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Device Comparison

| Parameter | Optic-Nerve Penetrating Array | Epiretinal Implant | Subretinal Implant | Thalamic (LGN) Implant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Anatomical Target | Optic nerve axons | RGC bodies/bipolar cells | Bipolar/RGC interface | LGN neurons |
| Number of Electrodes (state-of-art) | 20 – 100 | 60 (Argus II) | 1500 (PRIMA) | 8 – 24 (early) |
| Visual Field Coverage | Wide (whole optic nerve) | Central 20° | Central 10° | Wide |
| Surgical Risk | Moderate–High | Moderate | Moderate–High | High (craniotomy) |
| Phosphene Threshold | 10 – 200 μA | 10 – 100 μA | 10 – 50 μA | 5 – 80 μA |
| Applicable when Retina is Dead | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Major Clinical Investigators | C-Sight (Mexico), Veraart | Second Sight, Pixium | Pixium Vision | Multiple (early) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`
**Define optic nerve stimulation using penetrating microelectrode arrays. Mention any two advantages over retinal implants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Optic nerve stimulation using penetrating microelectrode arrays is a *visual neuroprosthetic technique* in which fine, needle-like electrodes (typically silicon-based, 1.0–1.5 mm in length) are inserted transversely into the **optic nerve (CN II)** to deliver charge-balanced electrical current pulses directly to the retinal ganglion cell axons, eliciting **phosphenes** — the elementary units of artificial vision.

**Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Bypasses the degenerated retina** — applicable in end-stage RP/AMD where the inner retina is also compromised, a condition that disqualifies epiretinal/subretinal implants.
2. **Wide visual field coverage** — a single penetrating array in the retrobulbar nerve can theoretically elicit phosphenes over a much larger fraction of the visual field compared to retinal prostheses limited to the macular region.
3. **Lower charge thresholds** — intraneural electrodes directly recruit axons at currents 5–10× lower than epiretinal or cuff electrodes.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`
**List the four main anatomical layers of the human visual pathway that a neuroprosthesis may target. State the specific cell type stimulated by a penetrating optic-nerve array.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four classical targets in the visual pathway, in anatomical order, are:
1. **Retina** (photoreceptors, bipolar cells, RGCs)
2. **Optic nerve** (retinal ganglion cell axons)
3. **Lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)** of the thalamus
4. **Primary visual cortex (V1)**

A **penetrating optic-nerve microelectrode array** stimulates the **retinal ganglion cell (RGC) axons** within the optic nerve (1.5 marks for the listing + 1.5 marks for correctly identifying RGC axons).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Answer any **ONE** full question. Each part carries 7 marks. Provide neat diagrams wherever specified.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **block-level architecture** of a percutaneous optic-nerve visual prosthesis. Briefly describe the role of the phosphene-mapping matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Weiss–Lapicque strength–duration equation** starting from the passive membrane model. From your result, obtain (i) the charge-threshold limit for short pulses and (ii) the chronaxie time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

*Block diagram description (5 marks for diagram + 1 mark for explanation + 1 mark for phosphene-mapping role):*

The complete functional architecture of a percutaneous optic-nerve prosthesis is best understood as a five-stage pipeline:

| Stage | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | External camera (CMOS, head-mounted) | Captures the scene in real time |
| 2 | Image pre-processor (DSP/FPGA) | Down-samples, grayscale, edge-detection |
| 3 | Phosphene-mapping matrix (RAM/lookup table) | Maps each image pixel to a specific electrode and stimulation amplitude |
| 4 | Current-controlled biphasic stimulator | Generates charge-balanced, cathodic-first current pulses |
| 5 | Penetrating microelectrode array (Utah/Michigan) | Delivers pulses to the optic nerve |

The **phosphene-mapping matrix** is a *learned calibration* established during early clinical sessions in which the patient verbally reports the perceived location and brightness of each elicited phosphene. This information is stored as a lookup table so that, in real time, any incoming image pixel can be translated to the *electrode–amplitude pair* that produces a phosphene at the desired retinotopic position. This step is essential because the **mapping between electrode location and phosphene location is non-linear** and varies widely across patients.

**(b) — 7 marks**

*Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above (5 marks for the derivation, 1 mark each for (i) and (ii)).*

(i) **Short-pulse charge threshold:** From Step 6 of Section 3.1, for $t \ll \tau_m$:

$$Q_{th} = I_{th} \cdot t \approx I_{rh} \cdot \tau_m = \text{constant}$$

(ii) **Chronaxie time:** From Step 8 of Section 3.1:

$$t_c = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \,\tau_m$$

The chronaxie is the pulse width that delivers threshold charge with minimum energy — a critical design parameter for chronic implants.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare **penetrating microelectrode arrays** with **cuff electrodes** for optic-nerve stimulation. Discuss, in particular, the differences in recruitment mechanism, threshold current, and selectivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Utah-style penetrating electrode has $I_{rh} = 25 \,\mu A$ and $\tau_m = 150 \,\mu s$. Determine: (i) the threshold current for a 300 μs pulse, (ii) the charge per phase, and (iii) whether the stimulation is within the **Shannon safety envelope**, given that the electrode tip area is $2000 \,\mu m^2$ and the safety limit is $\log_{10} D + 1.5 \log_{10} Q \le 1.85$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

| Property | Penetrating Array | Cuff Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | 10 × 10 silicon needles, 1.0–1.5 mm long | Ring/tube around the nerve |
| Electrode-tissue contact | Intraneural (inside perineurium) | Epineurial (outer surface only) |
| Threshold current | 10 – 200 μA | 0.5 – 5 mA |
| Recruitment mechanism | Direct depolarization of local axonal membrane | Volume-conducted field across the nerve |
| Selectivity (number of distinct phosphenes) | High (each tip can address a small axonal sub-population) | Low (current diffuses through whole nerve cross-section) |
| Surgical risk | Higher (nerve penetration, possible axonal damage) | Lower |
| Mechanical stability | Good (silicon shank is rigid) | Excellent (no penetration) |

*Mechanical stability and selectivity are the dominant trade-offs. Penetrating electrodes are preferred when fine, retinotopic phosphene maps are required (1 mark for each of the 6 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for concluding statement).*

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $I_{rh} = 25 \,\mu A$, $\tau_m = 150 \,\mu s$, $t = 300 \,\mu s$, $A_{elec} = 2000 \,\mu m^2 = 2 \times 10^{-6} \,cm^2$.

**(i) Threshold current for 300 μs pulse:**

$$I_{th} = \frac{25}{1 - e^{-300/150}} = \frac{25}{1 - e^{-2}} = \frac{25}{1 - 0.1353} = \frac{25}{0.8647} \approx 28.91 \,\mu A$$

**(ii) Charge per phase:**

$$Q = I_{th} \cdot t_{pw} = 28.91 \,\mu A \times 300 \,\mu s = 8673 \,\mu A \cdot \mu s = 8.673 \times 10^{-3} \,\mu C$$

In conventional units, $Q = 8.673 \,nC$ or $Q \approx 0.00867 \,\mu C$.

**(iii) Shannon safety check:**

Charge density:
$$D = \frac{Q}{A_{elec}} = \frac{8.673 \times 10^{-3} \,\mu C}{2 \times 10^{-6} \,cm^2} = 4336.5 \,\mu C/cm^2$$

Compute the Shannon index:
$$\log_{10}(4336.5) + 1.5 \times \log_{10}(0.00867)$$

$$= 3.637 + 1.5 \times (-2.062)$$

$$= 3.637 - 3.093 = 0.544$$

**Result:** $0.544 \le 1.85$ → **Stimulation is well within the safe envelope.** ✅

(Each sub-part 2 marks + 1 mark for safety conclusion with explicit numerical verification.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit-trap in (b)(ii):** Many students forget to convert $\mu m^2$ to $cm^2$ and obtain a charge density that is off by $10^8$, leading to a falsely "unsafe" verdict. *Always convert electrode area to cm² before dividing μC.*
> 2. **Pulse-width vs. period confusion:** Threshold is set by *pulse width* (in μs), not by *interpulse interval* (in ms). Do not substitute one for the other.
> 3. **In Q1, the four visual targets must be listed in anatomical order**; the examiner will not award the full 1.5 marks if the order is jumbled.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Definition** — Optic-nerve penetrating stimulation delivers biphasic charge-balanced current pulses to RGC axons via 1.0–1.5 mm silicon microelectrodes inserted *transversely* into CN II.
- **Bypass principle** — This technique works even when the retina is fully degenerated; the only anatomical requirement is survival of RGC axons.
- **Anatomical target list** — Retina → Optic nerve → LGN → V1 cortex (in order, memorize for 3-mark definition questions).
- **Three "big" formulas** — Weiss–Lapicque strength–duration, charge $Q = I \cdot t$, and Shannon safety $\log D + 1.5 \log Q \le 1.85$.
- **Chronaxie** is the *minimum-energy* pulse width; always equal to $0.693 \tau_m$.
- **Activating function** is the second spatial derivative of extracellular potential — recruitment is maximal where the field gradient is steepest, which is at the electrode **tip**.
- **Cuff vs penetrating** — Cuff: lower surgical risk but mA-level thresholds, low selectivity. Penetrating: μA-level thresholds, high selectivity, but higher surgical risk.
- **Clinical exemplars** — C-Sight (Mexico, 20-channel), Veraart's group, Lozano's penetrating arrays. Argus II / PRIMA are *retinal*, *not* optic-nerve, devices.
- **Number to remember** — Optic nerve has **~1.2 million** axons; safe per-phase charge density is **< 0.1 mC/cm²** for chronic use.
- **Most-missed concept** — *Phosphene-mapping* is patient-specific, non-linear, and must be re-calibrated periodically; it is the bridge between the engineering of the stimulator and the perceptual reality of the patient.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Neural Electrical Stimulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neural Electrical Stimulator (NES):** A *biomedical electronic device* that delivers precisely controlled, low-energy **electrical pulses** to *excitable neural tissue* (nerves, brain, spinal cord, or muscle) through **interfacing electrodes**, in order to *modulate, activate, or inhibit* neural activity for therapeutic, restorative, or functional purposes.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the Neural Electrical Stimulator is the **foundational hardware building block** of every neuroprosthetic system — from cochlear implants and retinal prostheses to deep brain stimulators (DBS), spinal cord stimulators (SCS), and functional electrical stimulators (FES).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Light Switch for Nerves"**
>
> Imagine a **fuse box** controlling the lights in a house. The fuse box does not "create" electricity for the bulbs — it only *delivers* the existing electricity in carefully measured bursts. A neural electrical stimulator works the same way for nerves: it does not create new nervous signals from nothing; instead, it **intercepts the biological wiring** and forces the nerve to fire a *new* action potential by injecting a small, controlled burst of charge.
>
> Just as a too-strong current would blow a fuse and a too-weak current would fail to switch on the bulb, a stimulator must deliver a charge that is **strong enough** to depolarize the neuron's membrane past its *threshold* (typically around **-55 mV**), but **small enough** to avoid electrochemical damage to the tissue.

### 1.3 The Big Picture — Why It Matters

- Every modern neuroprosthesis — cochlear implant, retinal implant, DBS, sacral nerve stimulator, FES for foot-drop — is **fundamentally a Neural Electrical Stimulator** plus an electrode interface.
- The "intelligence" of the device lies not in the stimulator alone but in the **closed-loop** combination: Stimulator → Electrode → Nerve → Response (sensor) → Controller → Stimulator.
- Design constraints are governed by **safety** (charge per phase **< 0.4 µC/cm²** for safe stimulation, known as the *Shannon limit*), **efficacy** (must recruit the target neurons), and **biocompatibility**.

### 1.4 The Five Core Parameters of Stimulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The 5 "Knobs" of a Stimulator**
>
> 1. **Amplitude (V or mA)** — how strong each pulse is.
> 2. **Pulse Width (µs or ms)** — how long each pulse lasts.
> 3. **Frequency (Hz)** — how many pulses are delivered per second.
> 4. **Waveform Shape** — monophasic vs. biphasic vs. more complex.
> 5. **Electrode Configuration** — monopolar, bipolar, multipolar.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Neural Stimulator

A neural stimulator is composed of **five interlinked functional blocks**. Understanding the role of each block is critical for any KTU exam question on "design of a stimulator."

1. **Power Source** — typically a *lithium-iodine* or *rechargeable lithium-ion* battery (3–6 V, 1–10 mAh typical).
2. **DC-DC Converter / Voltage Multiplier** — boosts the battery voltage to a higher rail (**5 V to 15 V**) so the output stage can drive the capacitive tissue load.
3. **Pulse-Forming Network (Output Stage)** — the *current-mirror* or *Howland current pump* that actually shapes the output pulse.
4. **Control / Logic Block** — a microcontroller, ASIC, or discrete logic that sets amplitude, pulse width, frequency, and timing.
5. **Electrode–Tissue Interface** — the lead wires, hermetic feedthrough, and electrode contacts in contact with the tissue.

### 2.2 Stimulus Waveforms — The Most Exam-Relevant Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monophasic vs. Biphasic Waveforms**
>
> - **Monophasic:** Single polarity pulse. *Net DC* flows through tissue → causes electrochemical damage and electrode corrosion. *Never used clinically for chronic implants.*
> - **Biphasic (Symmetric or Asymmetric):** A *leading cathodal* phase followed by an *anodal* phase. The two phases have **equal charge**, so net DC = 0. This is the **clinical gold standard**.
> - **Charge-Balanced Biphasic:** The *gold-standard* modern waveform. The second phase exactly cancels the charge injected by the first phase.

**Why biphasic is mandatory for chronic implants:**
- DC residue causes *electrolysis* of body fluids → produces H₂, O₂, Cl₂, and toxic species.
- DC residue drives *pH shifts* near the electrode.
- DC residue causes *faradaic* reactions that corrode the metal electrode.
- **Charge imbalance below 100 nC** is the modern safety target.

### 2.3 Strength–Duration Curve — The Most Tested Concept

The **Strength–Duration (S–D) curve** describes the *minimum stimulus amplitude* required to excite a nerve as a function of *pulse width*. It is a **hyperbolic** relationship governed by **Weiss's Law (1901)** and later refined by **Lapicque (1907)**.

The two most important landmarks on the curve are:
- **Rheobase (I_r):** The *minimum* current amplitude needed to excite the nerve at a *very long* pulse width. Conceptually, the DC threshold.
- **Chronaxie (C):** The *pulse width* at which the threshold current is **twice the rheobase**. Conceptually, a "characteristic time constant" of the nerve membrane.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula you need for KTU questions on Neural Electrical Stimulators.**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weiss's Law** | $I_{th} = a + \dfrac{b}{PW}$ | $I_{th}$ = threshold current, $a$ = constant, $b$ = constant, $PW$ = pulse width | mA, ms |
| 2 | **Lapicque Equation** | $I_{th} = I_{r}\left(1 + \dfrac{C}{PW}\right)$ | $I_{r}$ = rheobase, $C$ = chronaxie | mA, ms |
| 3 | **Charge per Phase** | $Q = I_{th} \cdot PW$ | $Q$ = charge | µC (micro-coulomb) |
| 4 | **Charge Density** | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}}$ | $A_{elec}$ = electrode area | µC/cm² |
| 5 | **Energy per Pulse** | $E = V \cdot I \cdot PW$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current | µJ |
| 6 | **Energy per Second** | $P = E \cdot f$ | $f$ = pulse frequency | µJ/s = µW |
| 7 | **Capacitive Discharge (voltage-mode)** | $V(t) = V_{0}\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau = R\,C_{tissue}$ | V, s |
| 8 | **Shannon Safety Limit** | $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) \le k$ | $D$ = charge density, $A_{elec}$ = electrode area, $k \approx 1.5$–$1.85$ | — |
| 9 | **Power Dissipated in Tissue** | $P = I^{2} R_{tissue}$ | $R_{tissue}$ = tissue resistance | mW |
| 10 | **Tissue RC Time Constant** | $\tau = R_{tissue}\cdot C_{tissue}$ | — | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Numericals**
> - **Safe charge injection limit:** $Q_{max} \approx 0.4\ \mu C / \text{phase}$ for small electrodes.
> - **Typical pulse widths:** $30$ to $400\ \mu s$.
> - **Typical clinical frequencies:** $20$ to $200\ Hz$.
> - **Typical neural time constant (τ):** $100\ \mu s$ to $1\ ms$.
> - **Chronaxie ranges:** $50\ \mu s$ (large myelinated) to $7\ ms$ (unmyelinated C-fibers).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DBS (Deep Brain Stimulator):** Frequency 130–185 Hz, PW 60–210 µs, amplitude 1–5 V, used for Parkinson's disease.
- **Cochlear Implant:** Frequency 250 Hz–8 kHz (channel-specific), PW ~25–100 µs, current-mode biphasic.
- **Spinal Cord Stimulator:** 40–60 Hz, PW 200–500 µs, used for chronic pain.
- **FES (Foot-Drop):** 20–50 Hz, PW 200–400 µs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Lapicque's Strength–Duration Equation from Membrane Dynamics

We model the **nerve membrane** as a simple *parallel RC circuit*, where:
- $C_m$ = membrane capacitance (charge-storage element of the lipid bilayer).
- $R_m$ = membrane resistance (ion-channel conductance).
- $V_m(t)$ = membrane potential.
- $I(t)$ = injected stimulus current.

**Step 1 — Write the KCL at the membrane node.**

The total current splits into a *resistive* component through $R_m$ and a *capacitive* component charging $C_m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(t) &= I_{R} + I_{C} \\[4pt]
I(t) &= \dfrac{V_m(t)}{R_m} + C_m\,\dfrac{dV_m(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "The current we inject must either flow out through the resistive channels or be stored on the membrane capacitor."

**Step 2 — Rearrange into the standard first-order linear ODE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_m\,\dfrac{dV_m}{dt} + \dfrac{V_m}{R_m} &= I(t) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{dV_m}{dt} + \dfrac{V_m}{R_m C_m} &= \dfrac{I(t)}{C_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Define the membrane time constant:* $\tau_m = R_m C_m$.

**Step 3 — Solve the homogeneous equation first.**

The natural (unforced) decay follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{m,h}(t) &= V_{0}\,e^{-t/\tau_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "If we leave the membrane alone, any pre-existing voltage $V_0$ decays exponentially with time constant $\tau_m$."

**Step 4 — Apply a rectangular stimulus pulse of constant amplitude $I$ for a duration $PW$.**

The particular (steady-state) solution of the ODE is $V_{m,p} = I R_m$.

The full solution under constant current (with the membrane initially at rest, $V_m(0)=0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m(t) &= I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the threshold condition.**

A neural action potential fires when $V_m(t)$ reaches the *threshold voltage* $V_{th}$. Setting $V_m(PW) = V_{th}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= I_{th}\,R_m \left(1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m}\right) \\[4pt]
I_{th} &= \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m \left(1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "To make the membrane voltage reach threshold exactly at the end of a pulse of width $PW$, the current must be high enough to overcome the exponential lag of the capacitor."

**Step 6 — Identify the limits (Rheobase and Chronaxie).**

- **Rheobase $I_r$:** As $PW \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes: $I_{th} \to V_{th}/R_m$. Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_r &= \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m}
\end{aligned}
$$
- **Chronaxie $C$:** The pulse width at which $I_{th} = 2 I_r$. Set $1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m} = 1/2$, giving $e^{-PW/\tau_m} = 1/2$, hence $PW = \tau_m \ln 2$. So:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m
\end{aligned}
$$
> *Textual interpretation:* "Chronaxie is approximately 70% of the membrane time constant — a quantitative link between stimulator design and nerve biophysics."

**Step 7 — Re-express in the classical Lapicque (Weiss) form.**

Substitute $V_{th}/R_m = I_r$ and rearrange the threshold equation to isolate $I_{th}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= \dfrac{I_r}{1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m}} \\[4pt]
\text{Using } e^{-x} &\approx 1 - x + \tfrac{x^2}{2} \text{ for small } x \text{ (Lapicque linearization):} \\[4pt]
I_{th} &\approx I_r\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_m}{PW}\right) = I_r\left(1 + \dfrac{C}{PW}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Lapicque Strength–Duration Equation**, the formula appearing in nearly every KTU question bank.

### 3.2 Derivation of Charge and Energy Equations

**Step 1 — For a rectangular pulse of constant amplitude $I_{th}$ and width $PW$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_{0}^{PW} I_{th}\,dt = I_{th}\cdot PW
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — For energy (assuming a constant electrode voltage $V$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{0}^{PW} V\cdot I_{th}\,dt = V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average power delivered at pulse rate $f$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= E \cdot f = V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "The total energy used by the stimulator per second is the energy of one pulse multiplied by the number of pulses per second — this is exactly what determines the *battery life* of an implanted device."

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A stimulator delivers biphasic rectangular pulses. Each cathodal phase has $I_{th} = 2.5\ mA$ and $PW = 200\ \mu s$, at a frequency of $50\ Hz$. The electrode area is $0.05\ cm^2$ and the compliance voltage is $9\ V$. Compute: (a) charge per phase, (b) charge density, (c) energy per pulse, (d) average power, and (e) check the Shannon safety criterion.

**Solution:**

**(a) Charge per phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I_{th} \cdot PW = (2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
Q &= 5.0 \times 10^{-7}\ C = 0.5\ \mu C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Charge density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}} = \dfrac{0.5\ \mu C}{0.05\ cm^2} = 10\ \mu C/cm^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Energy per pulse:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW = 9 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
E &= 4.5 \times 10^{-6}\ J = 4.5\ \mu J
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Average power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= E \cdot f = (4.5\ \mu J) \cdot 50\ Hz = 225\ \mu W
\end{aligned}
$$

**(e) Shannon safety check (using $k = 1.7$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) &= \log_{10}(10) + \log_{10}(0.05) \\[4pt]
&= 1.0 + (-1.301) = -0.301 \\[4pt]
&\le 1.7 \ \ \checkmark \ \text{(SAFE)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Verification conclusion:* The stimulation parameters lie **well within the safe region** of the Shannon plot, confirming clinical usability.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Voltage- vs. Current-Controlled Stimulators

| Property | Voltage-Controlled | Current-Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **Output variable held constant** | Voltage $V$ | Current $I$ |
| **Charge per pulse** | Depends on tissue impedance | Constant (independent of impedance) |
| **Safety against impedance change** | Poor (current can spike) | Excellent (current is fixed) |
| **Recruitment consistency** | Variable with scar formation | Stable with scar formation |
| **Implementation complexity** | Simple (just an analog switch) | Complex (Howland current source) |
| **Clinical use** | Pacemakers (older), TENS | Cochlear, DBS, FES, retinal |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Neural Stimulator

The following Mermaid diagram maps every functional block of a modern neural electrical stimulator, the signals between them, and the safety features that the KTU examiner typically tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    bat1["BATTERY 3 to 6 V"]
    dcdc1["DC DC CONVERTER 5 to 15 V"]
    ctrl1["CONTROL LOGIC MCU or ASIC"]
    parm1["PARAMETER SET AMPLITUDE PW FREQ"]
    pgen1["PULSE GENERATOR"]
    cur1["CURRENT MIRROR HOWLAND PUMP"]
    eleout["OUTPUT ELECTRODE"]
    elebias["RETURN ELECTRODE"]
    tis1["NEURAL TISSUE"]
    sen1["SENSING AMPLIFIER"]
    fb1["FEEDBACK SIGNAL"]
    safe1["CHARGE BALANCE MONITOR"]
    telem1["TELEMETRY LINK"]

    bat1 --> dcdc1
    dcdc1 --> pgen1
    ctrl1 --> parm1
    parm1 --> pgen1
    pgen1 --> cur1
    cur1 --> eleout
    elebias --> tis1
    eleout --> tis1
    tis1 --> sen1
    sen1 --> fb1
    fb1 --> ctrl1
    cur1 --> safe1
    safe1 --> ctrl1
    ctrl1 --> telem1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - Power flows from the **battery → DC-DC converter → pulse generator → current source → electrode → tissue**.
> - The **sensing amplifier** picks up the neural response (or impedance) and feeds it back to the controller for **closed-loop control**.
> - The **charge-balance monitor** continuously checks that the biphasic phases inject equal-and-opposite charge — this is the single most important **safety** feature of a clinical-grade stimulator.
> - The **telemetry link** is used to reprogram the implant wirelessly and to download diagnostic data.

### 4.2 Output Stage — Biphasic Current Pulse Waveform

The shape of the actual current waveform delivered to the tissue (captured in Mermaid as a stage-style schematic, since waveform plotting is best done conceptually).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0 START"]
    t1["CATHODAL PHASE -I PW"]
    t2["INTERPHASE DELAY 0 to 100 us"]
    t3["ANODAL PHASE +I PW"]
    t4["QUIET PERIOD 1 over f minus 2 PW"]
    t5["NEXT PULSE CYCLE"]
    t6["t = 1 over f REPEAT"]

    t0 --> t1
    t1 --> t2
    t2 --> t3
    t3 --> t4
    t4 --> t5
    t5 --> t6
    t6 --> t0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interphase delay** is a small zero-current gap (0–100 µs) inserted between the cathodal and anodal phases. It is used to ensure the **cathodal phase has fully depolarized the membrane** before the anodal phase reverses the field, improving the *selectivity* of stimulation.

### 4.3 Closed-Loop / Open-Loop Control Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    open1["OPEN LOOP FIXED PARAMETERS NO FEEDBACK"]
    clos1["CLOSED LOOP SENSOR FEEDS BACK TO CONTROLLER"]
    adv1["ADAPTIVE LOWER POWER LONGER BATTERY"]
    dis1["DISADVANTAGES COMPLEX HIGHER CURRENT DRAIN FROM SENSING"]

    open1 --> clos1
    clos1 --> adv1
    clos1 --> dis1
```

> Open-loop stimulators (e.g., older pacemakers) deliver fixed pulses regardless of the tissue state. Closed-loop (responsive) stimulators — like modern DBS devices and the new generation of cochlear implants — *adapt* to neural feedback, which **reduces battery use by 30–80%** in published clinical trials.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define a Neural Electrical Stimulator. List the five primary stimulus parameters that characterize its output.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Neural Electrical Stimulator (NES)** is an electronic device that delivers *precisely controlled electrical pulses* to *excitable neural tissue* (nerve, brain, spinal cord, or muscle) via electrodes, with the goal of *evoking, modulating, or inhibiting* neural activity for therapeutic or functional purposes.

The **five primary stimulus parameters** are:
1. **Amplitude** (V or mA) — strength of the pulse.
2. **Pulse width** (µs or ms) — duration of each phase.
3. **Frequency** (Hz) — pulse repetition rate.
4. **Waveform shape** — monophasic, biphasic, symmetric, charge-balanced.
5. **Electrode configuration** — monopolar, bipolar, multipolar, current-steering.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between monophasic and biphasic stimulation waveforms. Why is biphasic the clinical standard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Single (one direction) | Two (cathodal + anodal) |
| Net DC | Non-zero | Zero (if charge-balanced) |
| Tissue damage risk | High | Low |
| Electrode corrosion | Yes | No |
| Clinical use | Diagnostic only (EMG) | Therapeutic (chronic implants) |

**Why biphasic is the clinical standard (2 marks):**
- It **eliminates net DC current** → prevents electrolytic damage to tissue.
- It **prevents pH shifts** at the electrode–tissue interface.
- It **avoids faradaic reactions** that corrode metal electrodes.
- It is therefore **safe for chronic, long-term implantation**, as required for pacemakers, DBS, cochlear implants, etc.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEBT753]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a neural electrical stimulator**. Explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Lapicque Strength–Duration equation** starting from the equivalent RC model of a nerve membrane. Define *rheobase* and *chronaxie*, and explain their significance in choosing stimulus parameters. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Functional Architecture (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiners expect:* a labelled diagram (3 marks), a description of each block (3 marks), and a closing line on safety (1 mark).

A neural electrical stimulator consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **Power Source (Battery):** Provides a stable DC supply, typically a *lithium-iodine* primary cell (3–6 V) for non-rechargeable implants, or a *rechargeable lithium-ion* cell for rechargeable ones.
2. **DC-DC Converter / Voltage Multiplier:** Steps up the battery voltage to a higher rail (5–15 V) so that the output stage can drive the *capacitive* load of the tissue across the high-impedance hermetic feedthrough.
3. **Pulse Generator:** Produces a logic-level timing signal with the desired *pulse width* and *frequency*. Often implemented with a microcontroller, ASIC, or discrete timers (e.g., 555-based or digital counter).
4. **Output Stage (Current Source):** A **Howland current pump** or *Wilson current mirror* that converts the timing signal into a constant-current pulse, independent of tissue impedance. This is essential because the electrode–tissue impedance can vary by a factor of 2–10 over time due to fibrous encapsulation.
5. **Electrode–Tissue Interface:** The lead wires, hermetic feedthrough, and electrode contacts (Pt, Pt-Ir, IrOx) that physically deliver the current to the tissue.
6. **Control & Safety Circuitry:** Microcontroller that sets parameters, monitors charge balance (ensures the biphasic phases are equal in charge), and provides over-current and over-voltage protection.
7. **Telemetry (optional):** Inductive or RF link for wireless reprogramming and diagnostic data upload.

> *Closing line for full marks:* "Together, these blocks form a **charge-balanced, current-controlled, biphasic stimulator** — the safety-optimized architecture mandated by modern clinical neuroprosthetic design."

**Part (b) — Derivation of Lapicque Equation & Definition of Rheobase/Chronaxie (7 marks)**

The derivation is the same as the exhaustive 7-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Briefly recap:

1. Model the membrane as parallel $R_m \vert C_m$.
2. KCL gives: $I(t) = V_m/R_m + C_m \dfrac{dV_m}{dt}$.
3. Solve under constant current $I$ with $V_m(0) = 0$: $V_m(t) = I R_m (1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})$.
4. Threshold condition: $V_m(PW) = V_{th}$, so $I_{th} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m (1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m})}$.
5. **Rheobase** $I_r = V_{th}/R_m$ — the minimum DC current that can excite the nerve (achieved as $PW \to \infty$).
6. **Chronaxie** $C = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m$ — the pulse width that requires a current *twice* the rheobase.
7. Linearizing the exponential gives the **Lapicque equation**: $I_{th} = I_r(1 + C/PW)$.

**Significance in stimulus design (3 marks):**
- *Chronaxie* is the **optimal pulse width** for energy-efficient stimulation. Stimulating at $PW \approx C$ minimizes the total charge (and therefore battery energy) per pulse.
- *Rheobase* sets the **minimum amplitude** floor below which no pulse — however long — can excite the nerve.
- The S–D curve guides **selectivity**: a short PW selectively excites *large myelinated fibers* (low chronaxie) over *small unmyelinated fibers* (high chronaxie) — this is the *recruitment-order reversal* exploited in DBS and dorsal-column SCS.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEBT753]**
>
> **(a)** What is a **charge-balanced biphasic waveform**? Why is charge balance critical in chronic neural stimulation? Describe a typical biphasic pulse with the help of a clearly labelled amplitude-vs-time sketch. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A neural stimulator delivers rectangular cathodal pulses of amplitude $I_{th} = 3\ mA$ and pulse width $PW = 250\ \mu s$, at a frequency of $60\ Hz$, through an electrode of geometric area $0.06\ cm^2$. The compliance voltage is $10\ V$. Calculate the **(i)** charge per phase, **(ii)** charge density, **(iii)** energy per pulse, **(iv)** average power, and **(v)** verify the Shannon safety criterion (take $k = 1.7$). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Charge-Balanced Biphasic Waveform (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiners expect:* definition (2 marks), importance (3 marks), labelled sketch (2 marks).

A **charge-balanced biphasic waveform** is a stimulation pulse consisting of a *leading cathodal* phase (current flows from the electrode into the tissue, depolarizing the membrane) followed by an *anodal* phase of *equal and opposite* charge. The condition is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cathode} = Q_{anode} \quad &\Longleftrightarrow \quad I_{cathode} \cdot PW_{cathode} = I_{anode} \cdot PW_{anode}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Sketch (describe verbally, since we cannot draw graphs in Mermaid):*
> A time-axis $t$ (horizontal) and current-axis $I$ (vertical). The waveform goes **down** to $-I_{th}$ for a duration $PW$ (cathodal phase), then **up** to $+I_{th}$ for a duration $PW$ (anodal phase), then returns to zero. A small *interphase delay* (0–100 µs) may be shown between the two phases.

**Why charge balance is critical (3 marks):**
1. **Prevents electrolysis:** A net DC current would electrolyze body water → H₂, O₂, Cl₂ evolution → gas bubble formation, tissue disruption.
2. **Prevents pH shifts:** Faradaic reactions shift local pH → acidic or basic microenvironment → neuron death.
3. **Prevents electrode corrosion:** DC drives metal ions off the electrode → toxic metallic species, plus failure of the electrode itself.
4. **Regulatory compliance:** International standards (ISO 14708, FDA guidance) mandate charge imbalance < 100 nC per pulse for chronically implanted devices.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiners expect:* five sub-parts, each worth approximately 1.4 marks. Show formula, substitution, and final answer with units.

**Given:** $I_{th} = 3\ mA = 3 \times 10^{-3}\ A$, $PW = 250\ \mu s = 250 \times 10^{-6}\ s$, $f = 60\ Hz$, $A_{elec} = 0.06\ cm^2$, $V = 10\ V$, $k = 1.7$.

**(i) Charge per phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I_{th} \cdot PW = (3 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 7.5 \times 10^{-7}\ C = 0.75\ \mu C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Charge density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}} = \dfrac{0.75\ \mu C}{0.06\ cm^2} = 12.5\ \mu C/cm^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Energy per pulse:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW = 10 \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 7.5 \times 10^{-6}\ J = 7.5\ \mu J
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Average power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= E \cdot f = (7.5\ \mu J) \cdot 60\ Hz = 450\ \mu W = 0.45\ mW
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Shannon safety verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) &= \log_{10}(12.5) + \log_{10}(0.06) \\[4pt]
&= 1.097 + (-1.222) = -0.125 \\[4pt]
-0.125 &\le 1.7 \ \ \checkmark \ \text{(SAFE — well within the safe zone)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conclusion for full marks:* All five parameters satisfy the safety and engineering constraints — the design is clinically deployable.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to show units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. Always write *mA, µs, µC, µC/cm², µJ, µW*.
> 2. **Confusing "compliance voltage" with "stimulus voltage"** — Compliance voltage is the *maximum* the supply can deliver, not the voltage actually across the tissue. The actual voltage is $V = I_{th} \cdot Z_{tissue}$.
> 3. **Using `log₁₀` without parentheses in the Shannon equation** — examiners cannot read `log₁₀ D` ambiguously. Always write $\log_{10}(D)$.
> 4. **Calling a biphasic waveform "DC-free" without showing the calculation** — *charge balance* is a *quantitative* condition, not a verbal one. Show $Q_{cathode} = Q_{anode}$ explicitly.
> 5. **In the strength–duration derivation, stopping at the differential equation** — you must carry the derivation to the *Lapicque form* $I_{th} = I_r(1 + C/PW)$ to get full marks. The differential equation alone is worth only 2 of the 7 marks.
> 6. **Drawing a block diagram without arrows** — every block must be connected with a labelled, *directional* arrow indicating signal/power flow.
> 7. **Using $|x|$ notation inside markdown tables** — it breaks the table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX form (this is enforced by the KTU digital answer script parser).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **Neural Electrical Stimulator** is a current- or voltage-controlled electronic device that delivers pulses to excitable tissue via electrodes.
- [x] The **five primary stimulus parameters** are: amplitude, pulse width, frequency, waveform shape, and electrode configuration.
- [x] The **Lapicque (Strength–Duration) equation**: $I_{th} = I_r(1 + C/PW)$.
- [x] **Rheobase** = minimum DC threshold; **Chronaxie** ≈ 0.693 × membrane time constant.
- [x] **Charge per phase** $Q = I \cdot PW$; **Charge density** $D = Q/A_{elec}$.
- [x] **Energy per pulse** $E = V \cdot I \cdot PW$; **Average power** $P = E \cdot f$.
- [x] **Biphasic, charge-balanced** waveforms are the clinical standard for chronic implants.
- [x] **Monophasic** is never used chronically (causes tissue damage and electrode corrosion).
- [x] **Current-controlled** stimulators are preferred over voltage-controlled for safety and consistency.
- [x] **Shannon safety limit:** $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) \le 1.7$.
- [x] Typical safe charge per phase: $Q \le 0.4\ \mu C$.
- [x] Typical clinical pulse widths: $30$–$400\ \mu s$.
- [x] Typical clinical frequencies: $20$–$200\ Hz$.
- [x] Functional blocks: **Battery → DC-DC → Pulse Gen → Current Source → Electrode → Tissue → Sensor → Feedback**.
- [x] **Closed-loop (responsive) stimulators** reduce battery use by 30–80%.
- [x] **Interphase delay** improves selectivity by allowing the cathodal depolarization to take effect before the anodal reversal.
- [x] The **Howland current pump** is the most common current-source topology in modern implant stimulators.
